cards.text: A {{c1::statement}} is an instruction that tells the program to perform an action. Most statements end with a {{c2::semicolon}}.
A {{c1::function}} is a collection of statements that execute sequentially. Every C++ program must have a function called {{c2::main}}.
Single-line comments start with {{c1:://}} and multi-line comments are enclosed in {{c2::/*}} and {{c3::*/}}.
A {{c1::variable}} is a named region of memory. Variables must be {{c2::defined}} before use, and should be {{c3::initialized}} when defined.

cards.concept: The main initialization forms in modern C++
cards.explanation:
Modern C++ has several initialization forms, with list initialization (brace initialization) being preferred:
  • Copy initialization: int x = 5;
  • Direct initialization: int x(5);
  • List initialization: int x{5}; (preferred)
  • Value initialization: int x{}; (initializes to 0)

cards.text: Use {{c1::std::cout}} and {{c2::<<}} to output to console. Use {{c3::std::cin}} and {{c4::>>}} to read from keyboard.

cards.front: What are the three main ways to avoid undefined behavior from uninitialized variables?
cards.back:
• 1. Always initialize variables when defining them
  • 2. Use value initialization (empty braces) if you don't know the value yet
  • 3. Enable compiler warnings to catch uninitialized variable use

cards.concept: Rules and best practices for identifier names
cards.explanation:
Rules (must follow):
  • Can only contain letters, numbers, underscores
  • Cannot start with number
  • Cannot be a keyword
Best practices:
  • Use descriptive names
  • Start variables with lowercase
  • Avoid leading underscores
  • Be consistent with naming style

cards.text: A {{c1::literal}} is a fixed value inserted into source code, like {{c2::5}}, {{c3::3.14}}, or {{c4::"Hello"}}.
An {{c1::operator}} is a symbol that performs an operation. Operators can be {{c2::unary}} (one operand), {{c3::binary}} (two operands), or {{c4::ternary}} (three operands).

cards.front: What is an expression and what does it produce?
cards.back: An expression is a combination of literals, variables, operators, and function calls that is evaluated to produce a single value (called the result).

cards.explanation:
Essential practices for writing good C++ code:
  • Always initialize variables when defining them
  • Use list initialization (braces) for initialization
  • Write one statement per line for readability
  • Use descriptive identifier names
  • Add comments to explain why, not what
  • Format code consistently with proper indentation
  • Compile and test frequently (incremental development)
  • Enable compiler warnings
